coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Zheng
Yao, Meihuan
Niu, Xiaoqing
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description: A simple, low-cost and straightforward hydrothermal method for synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) has been developed via one-step pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid salt (EDTA) and thiourea. The prepared N, S-CDs had an average size of 2.45±0.37nm and showed a strong emission at the wavelength of 450nm with an optimum excitation of 390nm. Comparing to the other metal ions, mercury ions (Hg2+) dramatically quenched the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity displayed a good linear response with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–3.0×10−7 M (R 2 =0.9997) and the detection limit was 1.37×10−9 M. The cell viability results of Hela cells and the imaging experiments of Escherichia coli also certified that the N, S-CDs could be served as an effective fluorescent sensing probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in biological samples on account of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-CDs
Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid salt
Thiourea
Mercury ion
Fluorescent probe
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH and buffer systems effects on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs
Sensitivity and selectivity of the Hg2+ detection
Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Nature Science Foundation of China ( 30970696 and 21172056 ), Key Project of Henan Ministry of Education ( 14A150018 ), Key Programs of Henan for Science and Technology Development ( 142102310273 ), PCSIRT ( IRT1061 ), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Henan Province ( 2012IRTSTHN006 ), the analysis support from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Tsinghua University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different conditions of the synthesis were optimized: (a) optimization of the mass percentage of N and S; (b) optimization of synthesis temperature (from 100°C to 220°C); (c) optimization of heating time.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the N, S-CDs (a) and the corresponding size distribution (b). Inset: the HRTEM image of the N, S-CDs.
XRD spectra of the N, S-CDs.
FT-IR spectra of the N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption. (b) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the N, S-CDs in aqueous solutions, λ ex =390nm. Inset shows the photographs of the obtained N, S-CDs in aqueous solution under illumination of white (left) and UV light (365nm, right).
Excitation dependent emission spectra of the N, S-CDs.
XPS spectra of the obtained N, S-CDs. (a–d) High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, O1s, N1s and S2p of the N, S-CDs, respectively.

(a) pH effect on fluorescence emission of the N, S-CDs, The 0.1M HCl and NaOH solutions were used to tune pH. (b) Buffer systems effects on the fluorescence intensity under 390nm excitation wavelength.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N, S-CDs at different concentrations of Hg2+. C (Hg2+, ×10−6 M): 0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50, 5.0, 10.0, 30.0, 50.0, 80.0 (from top to bottom). (b) The dependence of FL intensity on the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of (0–80.0)×10−6 M, inset shows the linear relationship between the F 0/F and Hg2+ concentration. N, S-CDs: 0.01g/L; λ ex =390nm.

(a) The fluorescence responses of the N, S-CDs by adding different metal ions with the same concentration of 3.00×10−5 M. (b) The corresponding fluorescence intensities before (red) and after (green) the treatment with Hg2+ (3.00×10−5 M). N, S-CDs: 0.01g/L; λ ex =390nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this legend, the reader is referred to the web version of the article.)
MTT based cytotoxicity assay of HeLa cells after 24h incubation with different concentrations of N, S-CDs (0, 0.125, 0.250, 0.500, 1.00 and 2.00g/L) (mean±SD, n =5).

Images of Escherichia coli incubated with N, S-CDs for 18h at 37°C without (a, b) and with (c) Hg2+ obtained under bright field (a) and fluorescence (b, c) at 405nm excitation. N, S-CDs: 0.10g/L; Hg2+: 5.00×10−6 M; the magnification was ×40.
Illustration of the formation process of N, S co-doped CDs via one-step hydrothermal of EDTA and thiourea and their application to Hg2+ analysis.
Comparison of different fluorescent nanoparticles-based methods for Hg2+ detection.
Determination and recovery tests of Hg2+ in real samples by the present method (n =5).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Green Chemical Media and Reactions, Ministry of Education, Henan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical Engineering, Henan Mechanical and Electrical Engineering College, Xinxiang 453000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical Engineering, Henan Mechanical and Electrical Engineering College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, low-cost and straightforward hydrothermal method for synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) has been developed via one-step pyrolysis of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid salt (EDTA) and thiourea. The prepared N, S-CDs had an average size of 2.45±0.37nm and showed a strong emission at the wavelength of 450nm with an optimum excitation of 390nm. Comparing to the other metal ions, mercury ions (Hg2+) dramatically quenched the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity displayed a good linear response with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–3.0×10−7 M (R 2 =0.9997) and the detection limit was 1.37×10−9 M. The cell viability results of Hela cells and the imaging experiments of Escherichia coli also certified that the N, S-CDs could be served as an effective fluorescent sensing probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in biological samples on account of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of modern technology, mercury ion is widely used in industry. Now the total global amount of anthropogenic mercury released into the atmosphere is about 3000tons per year [1]. As the most toxic heavy metal ion, it cannot be destroyed due to its non-biodegradation and accumulation [2], which is enriched in food, water and environment and is absorbed by humans. In the USA the maximum contamination limit for Hg2+ is 2ppb and 1ppb in the European Union [3], so it is very significant to develop facile and accurate Hg2+ detection methods. The traditional measurement methods of Hg2+ include atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), surface-enhanced Raman scattering, polarography, electrochemical methods and so on [4–8]. However, these methods usually need sophisticated and expensive instrument or complicated sample treatment. In recent years, nanoparticle-based fluorescent probes such as gold and silver nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots and carbon nanoparticles have been utilized for Hg2+ detection due to their fast analysis, high sensitivity and selectivity [9–12]. But these fluorescence probes also suffer from complex synthesis, high cost, toxicity, poor solubility in water and low biological compatibility. Hence finding new Hg2+ fluorescent probes is urgent to overcome the limitations.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, were first discovered in 2004 [13]. They are the nanomaterials with the size below 10nm and carbon as the basic structural unit and can be dispersed in water or the other solvents with spherical shape. Besides, they also have unique optical properties, high photostability, low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility [14] that have attracted much attention for their promising applications in biological imaging [15], biological sensor [16], disease diagnosis [17], targeted tracer [18] and drug release/delivery [19,20]. Now, CDs have also been used as fluorescent probes for sensing metal ions, such as mercuric ions. Yu's group developed a label-free C-dots probe for Hg2+ determination by hydrothermal method using a plant of Jinhua bergamot as a carbon source [21]. Helena GonÇalve's group obtained Hg (II) sensing based on carbon dots functionalized with NH2-polyethylene-glycol and N-acetyl-l-cysteine by direct laser ablation [22]. Furthermore, they also applied sol–gel method and layer-by-layer assembly method immobilization of the functionalized carbon dots on optical fibers as optical fluorescence sensors for detection of Hg2+ ions respectively [23,24], etc. In general, carbon dots are mainly synthesized through two kinds of methods: physical and chemical methods. Physical synthesis always refers to arc discharge [13], laser ablation [25], plasma synthesis [26], etc. Chemical methods include electrochemical method [27], hydrothermal method [28], combustion/strong acid oxidation method [29,30], microwave [31] and ultrasonic synthetic method [32]. However, most of the synthesized bare CDs via these methods have low fluorescent quantum yield in comparison to the semiconductor quantum dots. Doped CDs was proved to be a promising method for highly fluorescent dots [33]. In past years extensive efforts have been made for preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs [34,35]. Recently, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped CDs have also received great attention. Sun et al. synthesized sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots taking hair fiber as a precursor with tunable luminescence properties [36]; Qu et al. synthesized highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots through citric acid and thiourea for visible light photocatalyst [37]; Wang et al. used glutathione [38]; Dong et al. applied l-cysteine and citric acid [39]; and Guo et al. employed sulfate-reducing bacteria treating graphene oxide for prepared S, N co-doped carbon dots [40]. But, many of the assays have some defects such as applying much concentrated acid, difficult to control particle size, and complex post-treatment which limit their application in routine analysis. Thus, developing a facile, low-cost, and high-yield method for the preparation of N, S-CDs with strong fluorescent emission and novel properties is very meaningful.

EDTA has been extensively used as chelating reagent for having much NH2, COOH in its structural formula. Thiourea also contains functional groups: NH2 and sulfur, which have been considered as necessary groups to produce high quantum yield of CDs. So we prepared the N, S-CDs for the first time by one-step hydrothermal treatment using EDTA and thiourea as substrates. The obtained N, S-CDs owned high luminescence (quantum yield 35.2%). Without any further chemical modification, the efficient quenching of the fluorescence carbon dots by metal ions was studied. The experiments showed that the fluorescence of N, S-CDs was obviously quenched only by Hg2+ even at fairly low concentrations. This meant that a high selective and sensitive fluorescence probe had been built for the detection of trace Hg2+ (Scheme 1 ).

EDTA and thiourea were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China) and were used without further purification. The other chemicals in this work were all of analytical reagents from commercial suppliers and used directly. Inorganic salts Mg(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2, AgNO3, Co(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, Ni(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2 and NaCl, KCl, ZnCl2, AlCl3, BaCl2, MnCl2, FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved in phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 1.00×10−2 M, pH=7.4) to afford 1.00×10−2 M cation solutions. PBS as the buffer solution and ultrapure water from a Milli-Q ultrapure water system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) were used throughout the experiment.

The size and microstructures of N, S-CDs were examined using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEOLJEM-2010 electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV by dropping the sample dispersion onto a carbon-coated copper grid and drying at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted with Spectrum 400F spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) using KBr pellets technique ranging from 500 to 4000cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Puxi Analytic Instrument Ltd., Beijing, China). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (VARIAN, USA) applying a quartz cuvette of 2mm path length at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 500nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried on a D8 Advance automatic powder diffractometer (Bruker, German) employing CuKα radiation. The fluorescence images were taken on a Leica laser confocal fluorescence microscope (TCSSP5).

3.00g EDTA was dissolved into 30mL ultrapure water and stirred to form a clear solution. Following the solution was sealed into a 50mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180°C for 10h in air dry oven. The resulting solution was cooled down to room temperature naturally and centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 20min to discard non-fluorescent deposit. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22μm filter membrane to remove large particles. The pure solution was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60°C for 24h and stored at 4°C.

3.00g EDTA and 1.53g thiourea were dissolved into 30mL ultrapure water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was sealed into a 50mL Teflon-equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180°C for 10h in air dry oven. The resulting brown yellow solution was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged at 15,000rpm for 20min. The supernatant containing N, S-CDs was filtered through a 0.22μm filter membrane. The pure solution was dried at 60°C for 24h and stored at 4°C for characterization. The dried N, S-CDs powder was dissolved in ultrapure water with a concentration of 0.01g/L.

The quantum yield (QY) of N, S-CDs was calculated according to the following equation.
(1)
Q x
=
Q s
I x
I s
A s
A x
η x
η s
2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the emission intensity of fluorescence spectra, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the absorbance at the excited wavelength. The subscript “s” represents the standard fluorophore of known quantum yield and “x” for the sample. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm) was chosen as a standard. In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, all absorbance in the 1.0cm quartz cuvette were kept under 0.1at the excitation wavelength of 360nm.

100μL aqueous solution of N, S-CDs and different amounts of Hg2+ were added in a series of 2.0mL centrifuge tubes. The mixtures were diluted to 1.0mL with PBS solution and fully mixed. The fluorescence spectra were received after reaction for 20min with an excitation at 390nm. The sensitivity of Hg2+ ion was carried out using different concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 80.0 (×10−6 M). The selectivity for Hg2+ was confirmed by adding the above metal ions in the absence and presence of Hg2+ in a similar way. The fluorescence intensity was gained with excitation wavelength of 390nm and all experiments were performed at room temperature.

HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well microtiter plates at a density of 5×104 per well and adhered for 24h at 37°C in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 5% CO2, followed by addition of the N, S-CDs suspensions at different concentrations and cultured for another 24h. After adding of 20μL 5.0g/L 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) solution into every cell well, the cells were further incubated for 4h, then the culture medium was removed and 150μL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was added. Shaking the resulting mixture for 15min in the dark at room temperature, its optical density (OD) was measured at 570nm on a microplate reader (Biorad) and the values were compared to the control cells.

The feasibility of the N, S-CDs as bioimaging agents was explored using Escherichia coli as a model. The E. coli were incubated in nutrient broth at 37°C. Then the N, S-CDs (the final N, S-CDs concentration of 0.10g/L) was added into the medium for 18h of incubation. After uptake, the E. coli were supplemented with Hg2+ (5.00×10−6 M) in the growth medium for another 4h. The particles were detected by confocal fluorescence microscope.

To obtain optimized synthesis conditions, the mass percentage of N and S, synthesis temperature and heating time were discussed for preparing N, S-CDs. The results (Fig. 1a–c) revealed that the fluorescent intensities changed along with varying the reaction conditions. As shown in Fig. 1a, the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs increased with the mass percentage of N and S at the range of 19.6–24.3% and almost kept unchanged from 28.2 to 34.6% reaching the maximal value, then decreased with the increasing percentage ranging from 37.2 to 39.6% which might be ascribed to the aggregation of the synthesized CDs resulting in the fluorescence quenching. So 31.6%, that is to say, the molar ratio of EDTA and thiourea was 1:2.5 being selected for the synthesis condition. 180°C and 10h were also chosen for the optimal conditions.

The TEM image of prepared N, S-CDs displayed in Fig. 2a and b indicating that they had good monodispersity and small particle size with the size of 2.45±0.37nm (mean±SD, n =5) by measuring 100 random nanoparticles. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2a inset) provided the obvious lattice planes with an interfringe distance of 0.34nm, which is in good agreement with that of graphite facets (002 plane, at 2θ =23.5°), as strongly supported by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 3 ). The results meant that the nanoparticles might be composed of nanocrystalline cores of graphitic sp2 carbon atoms.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy identified the surface functional groups of the synthesized N, S-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 4 , the broad absorption bands at 3413 and 3177cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of OH and NH respectively. The band at 1631cm−1 was from the bending vibrations of CO. These suggested that there were many amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of N, S-CDs which made the synthesized N, S-CDs excellent water-solubility. The peak at 1493 and 1398cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibrations of CC and CH, NH, CS, CN, respectively. CO bending vibrations appeared at 1195cm−1 and the peak at 1051cm−1 was related to CS bending vibrations. Weak CS stretching observed at 862cm−1. From the FT-IR analysis it can be proved that nitrogen and sulfur atoms are successfully doped in the structure of N, S-CDs.

XPS results explored the composition and the chemical bonding of the N, S-CDs. In the spectrum (Fig. 7 ) of the N, S-CDs there were three dominant peaks at 532.0eV (O1s), 400.5eV (N1s), and 284.4eV (C1s). Furthermore, there were two weak peaks at 162.8 and 226.6eV, which belonged to S2p and S2s, respectively. For the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 7a) there were five peaks observed at 284.5, 285.8, 287.7, 288.3 and 288.9eV, which were attributed to the CC/CC, CS, CN, CO and CO/CS groups, respectively [11,18]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 7b) showed two peaks at 531.3 and 534.5eV, which were attributed to the CO and OH groups, respectively. The spectrum of N1s (Fig. 7c) indicated two peaks at 400.4eV and 399.7eV, which were assigned to NH and CN. Besides, the S2p pattern (Fig. 7d) could be observed three peaks at 162.0, 163.2 and 168.5eV, which were close to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 positions of the CS and CS covalent bonds [43], in accord with the FT-IR analysis. Additionally, XPS results also displayed that the N, S-CDs nanomaterials contained 52.7%, 9.1%, 24.9%, 2.7% of C, N, O and S, respectively, demonstrating nitrogen and sulfur were successfully doped in the structure of N, S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and buffer systems effects on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the optical properties of CDs arise from particle surface states. Different pH values may have distinct effects for the fluorescence intensity of CDs. For seeking an optimal pH value for the following experiment, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs was researched. The results (Fig. 8a) showed that the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs first increased with raising pH from 2 to 5. When pH varied from 6 to 8 it almost kept unchanged reaching its maximal value and then decreased with pH further increased. This phenomenon may be due to the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of N, S-CDs in acidic and alkali media. But in the higher pH mercury ions easily hydrolyze, pH 7.4 was chosen for the Hg2+ ions detection. In order to obtain a better detection for Hg2+ ion, the effects of buffer systems on fluorescence intensity were surveyed at the same pH (pH 7.4) using four buffer solutions including Tris–HCl buffer, Tris–HAc buffer, HAc-NaAc buffer and PBS buffer. The maximal FL intensity was gained in PBS buffer (Fig. 8b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
To discuss the sensitivity of the system, different concentrations of Hg2+ from 0 to 80.0 (×10−6 M) were added to the aqueous solution of N, S-CDs and the fluorescence emission intensity was measured. As shown in Fig. 9a, at around 450nm the FL intensity of the N, S-CDs decreased gradually with increasing Hg2+ concentration, implying that adding Hg2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs. Meanwhile, in Fig. 9b the fluorescence intensity showed a good linear response with Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–3.0×10−7 M. The Stern–Volmer equation was F 0/F =1.0009+0.9248 [Hg2+] (R 2 =0.9997, F 0 and F were the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the Hg2+, respectively.), the Stern–Volmer quenching constant was 9.25×105 L/mol. Under this experiment condition, the limit of detection (LOD) for Hg2+ was calculated to be 1.37×10−9 M according to the equation.
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / s
where σ was the standard deviation of the N, S-CDs blank signals (n =20, λ ex =390nm) and s was the slope of the calibration plot. This value of LOD was much below the maximum level of Hg2+ ions permitted (1.0×10−8 M) by the Environmental Protection Agency (USA) for drinking water and was much lower than those of previously reported carbon dots based nanosensors (Table 1 ) [3,12,21,28,38,44–48]. The sensing system was much more sensitive toward Hg2+ ions.

The selectivity of the N, S-CDs toward Hg2+ was also examined. Different metal ions such as Cu2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cd2+ and Fe2+ ions were added into the probe solution. The results in Fig. 10a manifested that Fe2+ had almost no interference on the fluorescence intensity of the N, S-CDs, but the fluorescence intensity was observably quenched by Hg2+ and reduced about 70%. By contrast FL signal changes were decreased less than 5% by injecting other ions into the N, S-CDs solution under the same conditions. However the fluorescence intensity was sharply decreased by further treatment with the same concentration of Hg2+ (3.00×10−5 M) (Fig. 10b). The N, S-CDs-based FL sensor was highly selective toward Hg2+ over the other metal ions. The fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent CDs maybe result from the chelating reaction of Hg2+ with N, S-CDs through sulfur and COO− groups on the N, S-CDs surface, which is just as the literature reported [47,49,50]. The strong binding between Hg2+ and S may be the major reason.

Before biological applications, the MTT assay was conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of N, S-CDs using Hela cells as representative cell lines. The results showed in Fig. 11 certified that 96.4, 94.5, 92.7% of the cells were viable when incubated with 0.125, 0.250, 0.500g/L N, S-CDs for 24h respectively. Even the concentration of N, S-CDs increased to 1.00g/L and 2.00g/L, the corresponding cell viability only decreased to 86.8% and 85.4% respectively, most cells were still alive even at the such high concentrations confirming the particles low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. Each experiment was repeated for 5 times, RSD<2.86%.

In view of its low cytotoxicity and good water-solubility, we explored the feasibility of the N, S-CDs as bioimaging agent using E. coli as a model. The small size and water-soluble nature of the N, S-CDs made them easily permeated into the E. coli. When the E. coli were incubated with the N, S-CDs for 18h at 37°C, the high fluorescence intensity clearly illustrated the morphology of the E. coli (Fig. 12b) indicating that a large amount of the N, S-CDs had been internalized into the E. coli and they had good biocompatibility. Afterwards dropping Hg2+ at a low concentration (5.00×10−6 M) in the growth medium the fluorescence intensities of the E. coli were significantly fallen (Fig. 12c). These experiments meant that the N, S-CDs could be served as an effective fluorescent sensing probe for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in biological samples on account of their low toxicity and good biocompatibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to appraise the feasibility of the proposed method, different real water samples such as mineral water, tap water and ground water were acquired from the supermarket, our laboratory and the well in Henan normal university. The concentrations of Hg2+ in these samples were detected by the present method and flame atomic absorption spectrometry method (FAAS) without any pretreatment. The samples were determined by the standard addition method and the analytical results were summarized in Table 2 . The results certified that there were no Hg2+ in all the real samples, these results received by the present procedure were consistent with the results by the FAAS method. In addition, the good recoveries of Hg2+ in the range of 96.48–105.2% were also attained with RSD of around 1.82% (n =5). The results definitely demonstrated the accuracy and reliability of the present method for Hg2+ detection in practical samples.

Doping is an essential strategy to improve photoluminescence properties of carbon dots. In this work, we synthesized the highly photoluminescent N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots for the first time using EDTA and thiourea as precursors by a facile, green, and one-step hydrothermal method with a quantum yield of 35.2%. The prepared N, S-CDs had a similar particle size and strong fluorescence intensity which was dependent on excitation wavelength from 340 to 500nm. Without further chemical modification, the N, S-CDs were shown to be a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor for Hg2+ with a detection limit of 1.37×10−9 M. The mechanism of Hg2+ detection was also explored based on the fluorescence quenching of photoluminescent C-dots resulting from the chelating reaction of Hg2+ with N, S-CDs through S and COO− groups on the N, S-CDs surface. The study provided a new and effective Hg2+ sensor in biological and environmental samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Nature Science Foundation of China (30970696 and 21172056), Key Project of Henan Ministry of Education (14A150018), Key Programs of Henan for Science and Technology Development (142102310273), PCSIRT (IRT1061), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Henan Province (2012IRTSTHN006), the analysis support from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Tsinghua University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of mercury ions (Hg2+) in urine using a terbium chelate fluorescent probe
A highly selective fluorescent probe for Hg2+ based on a rhodamine–coumarin conjugate
Carbon dots functionalized by organosilane with double-sided anchoring for nanomolar Hg2+ detection
Determination of total mercury in human hair and animal fur by combustion atomic absorption spectrometry
Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions
Highly sensitive SERS detection of Hg2+ ions in aqueous media using gold nanoparticles/grapheme heterojunctions
A polarographic study of chlorogenic acid and its interaction with some heavy metal ions
Anodic stripping voltammetric detection of mercury (II) using Au-PEDOT modified carbon paste electrode
Highly sensitive detection of mercury (II) ions by fluorescence polarization enhanced by gold nanoparticles
Ultrasensitive and dual functional colorimetric sensors for mercury (II) ions and hydrogen peroxide based on catalytic reduction property of silver nanoparticles
Detection of toxic mercury ions using a ratiometric CdSe/ZnS nanocrystal sensor
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting hydrogen sulfide in aqueous media and inside live cells
Polyamidoamine dendrimers-capped carbon dots/Au nanocrystal nanocomposites and its application for electrochemical immunosensor

Self-assembled hybrids of fluorescent carbon dots and PAMAM dendrimers for epirubicin delivery and intracellular imaging
Green preparation of carbon dots by Jinhua bergamot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Layer-by-layer immobilization of carbon dots fluorescent nanomaterials on single optical fiber
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
New strategy for synthesis and functionalization of carbon nanoparticles
One-pot electrochemical synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and their selective sensing for mercury ion
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Hollow luminescent carbon dots for drug delivery
One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles directly from active carbon via a one-step ultrasonic treatment
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
One-step preparation of nitrogen-doped grapheme quantum dots from oxidized debris of grapheme oxide
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Solvent-free synthesis of sulfur-and nitrogen-co-doped fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from glutathione for highly selective and sensitive detection of mercury (II) ions
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
One-pot microbial method to synthesize dual-doped graphene and its use as high-performance electrocatalyst
One-step synthesis of amino-functionalized fluorescent carbonnanoparticles by hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
Water-soluble, nitrogen-doped fluorescent carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution
Synthesis of carbon nanoparticles using one step green approach and their application as mercuric ion sensor
Preparation of photoluminescent carbon nanodots by traditional Chinese medicine and application as a probe for Hg2+
One-pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
Turn-on fluorescent chemosensor based on an amino acid for Pb (II) and Hg (II) ions in aqueous solutions and role of tryptophan for sensing
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury (II) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zheng Fu is a lecturer in the Department of Electrical Engineering at Henan Mechanical and Electrical Engineering College (PR China). Now she is a graduate student in the Henan Normal University, under the supervision of Professor Fengling Cui. Her research interests include the surface functionalization of carbon dots and their application in the sensing and bioimaging.

Meihuan Yao is a lecturer at Henan Normal University (PR China). She received her PhD degree from Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2010. Her research interests focus on synthesis of carbon nanomaterials and their application in the sensing.

Xiaoqing Niu is a graduate student in the Henan Normal University (PR China), under the supervision of Professor Fengling Cui. Her research interests are the green synthesis of doped carbon dots and their application in the sensing and bioimaging.

Fengling Cui is a professor in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Henan Normal University (PR China). She received her PhD degree from Lanzhou University in 2004. Her main research fields include the design and synthesis of coordination compounds and fluorescent probes.
